title: Support for environmental protection: an integration of ideological-consistency and information-deficit models
authors: Phillip J. Ehret; Aaron C. Sparks; David K. Sherman

tax_mentions.context: Mentioned only as an example environmental issue; the study measures general environmental support and climate change belief.
tax_mentions.notes: No carbon/fuel tax approval or preference item is analyzed.

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: increase federal spending on the environment
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Assesses support for higher federal environmental spending, not preference for a carbon or fuel tax instrument.

excluded_policy_mentions.exact_term_used_in_paper: protect the environment even if it means losing jobs
excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Measures a general environmental protection job–environment tradeoff, not acceptability of carbon/fuel taxation.

inclusion_reason: No direct public acceptability/support analysis for carbon or fuel taxes; outcomes are general environmental protection support and climate change belief.
exclusion_reason: Environmental attitudes are measured as preferences for protecting the environment or increasing federal environmental spending, not support for a carbon/fuel tax or TICPE-like instrument.
